summary: Turns on the LLTD Mapper I/O driver through the official Machine policy path. Microsoft directly documents the same policy key, value name, and 1/0 semantics that the app uses for this LLTD Mapper I/O setting.

setting.name: LLTD Mapper I/O Driver Policy
setting.casual_explanation: LLTD helps Windows map and discover devices on a local network. The Mapper I/O side lets the PC ask the network about its topology.
setting.why_people_change_it: People usually change this only when they want to force Windows network mapping features on, or when they are troubleshooting discovery behavior.
setting.tradeoffs_overview: Turning this policy on can improve local network mapping features, but it is not a general performance tweak and many people never notice the difference.
setting.professional_notes: Microsoft documents EnableLLTDIO under HKLM\Software\Policies\Microsoft\Windows\LLTD. When the policy is disabled or not configured, default LLTDIO behavior applies instead of a hard feature-off state.

allowed_values.label: Default behavior
allowed_values.meaning: Windows uses the default LLTD Mapper I/O behavior because the policy is not configured.

allowed_values.label: Policy disabled
allowed_values.meaning: The administrative template policy is disabled, which reverts to default LLTD Mapper I/O behavior rather than publishing a hard feature-off state.

allowed_values.label: Policy enabled
allowed_values.meaning: The LLTD Mapper I/O driver is explicitly turned on by policy.

targets.notes: Additional allow and prohibit booleans exist, but the current tweak only controls the main enable value.

app_current_implementation.notes: The current app writes EnableLLTDIO = 1, which matches the documented policy enable state.

windows_defaults.label: Policy not configured

states.rationale: Microsoft says the default LLTDIO behavior applies when the policy is not configured.

windows_defaults.notes: The policy disabled state also falls back to default behavior instead of defining a separate hard-off mode.

recommended_profiles.label: Windows default
intended_for: People who do not manage LLTD explicitly
avoid_for: Admins who intentionally want to force LLTD Mapper I/O on

states.rationale: Leave the policy unconfigured unless you specifically need to force LLTD behavior.

recommended_profiles.good_when: You want normal Windows behavior without forcing a network discovery policy.
recommended_profiles.bad_when: You are trying to enforce LLTD availability for a managed scenario.
tradeoffs: No explicit policy override is set.
Behavior stays closer to the Windows baseline.

recommended_profiles.label: Force enable LLTDIO
intended_for: Managed environments that want LLTD Mapper I/O forced on
avoid_for: People looking for a generic network performance tweak

states.rationale: This is the explicit policy enable state Microsoft documents.

recommended_profiles.good_when: You specifically need LLTD Mapper I/O enabled by policy.
recommended_profiles.bad_when: You are changing it without a clear discovery or troubleshooting goal.
tradeoffs: Network mapping behavior is forced on by policy.
This is not a universal optimization for all users.

evidence.title: Microsoft administrative template for LLTD Mapper I/O
evidence.summary: The LinkLayerTopologyDiscovery administrative template documents EnableLLTDIO at HKLM\Software\Policies\Microsoft\Windows\LLTD with enabled value 1 and disabled value 0.

evidence.title: Microsoft help text for LLTD Mapper I/O policy
evidence.summary: Microsoft explains that enabling the policy turns on LLTD Mapper I/O behavior and that disabling or not configuring the policy leaves default behavior in place.

evidence.title: Current network tweak provider
evidence.summary: The current app writes EnableLLTDIO = 1 for this tweak.

decision.why: Microsoft directly documents HKLM\Software\Policies\Microsoft\Windows\LLTD -> EnableLLTDIO in the LinkLayerTopologyDiscovery administrative template, with enabledValue 1 and disabledValue 0. The companion ADML help text explains that enabling the policy turns on Mapper I/O behavior and disabling or not configuring it leaves default behavior in place.

validation_proof.exact_quote_or_path: <policy name="LLTD_EnableLLTDIO" class="Machine" ... key="Software\Policies\Microsoft\Windows\LLTD" valueName="EnableLLTDIO"> ... <enabledValue><decimal value="1" /></enabledValue> ... <disabledValue><decimal value="0" /></disabledValue>; LinkLayerTopologyDiscovery.adml: If you disable or do not configure this policy setting, the default behavior of LLTDIO will apply.
validation_proof.notes: The local official Microsoft administrative template defines the exact LLTD policy path and value name used by the app, and the companion ADML help text documents the enable/default behavior.
